naturalMeaning: The safe all-purpose 'hello' that works with any listener; in warm everyday speech Vietnamese usually replace it with 'chào' plus a kinship word.

examples.text: Xin chào, tôi tên là Nam.
examples.translation: Hello, my name is Nam.

naturalMeaning: You almost never say 'chào' alone; you pick the address word for the listener. 'Anh' is for a man a bit older than you (or any adult man you want to flatter slightly).

examples.text: Chào anh, anh khỏe không?
examples.translation: Hi, how are you?

naturalMeaning: 'Chị' addresses a woman somewhat older than you; the polite default for most adult women in shops and offices.

examples.text: Chào chị, chị cần gì ạ?
examples.translation: Hi, what would you like?

naturalMeaning: 'Em' addresses anyone clearly younger than you, including waiters, students and children; friendly, not condescending.

examples.text: Chào em, em học lớp mấy?
examples.translation: Hi, what grade are you in?

naturalMeaning: 'Bạn' means 'friend' and is the neutral choice for someone your own age when you don't know them yet.

examples.text: Chào bạn, rất vui được gặp bạn.
examples.translation: Hi, nice to meet you.

naturalMeaning: A slightly formal 'goodbye'; casually people just say 'chào anh/chị' again on the way out.

examples.text: Tạm biệt, hẹn gặp lại nhé.
examples.translation: Goodbye, see you again.

naturalMeaning: Literally 'appointment to meet again'; the standard warm sign-off.

examples.text: Hẹn gặp lại tuần sau!
examples.translation: See you again next week!

naturalMeaning: Said at bedtime or when parting for the night; literally 'wish (you) sleep well'.

examples.text: Muộn rồi, chúc ngủ ngon nhé.
examples.translation: It's late, good night.

naturalMeaning: Swap the final address word for your listener ('...gặp chị/em/bạn'). Literally 'very happy to be able to meet you'.

examples.text: Tôi là David. Rất vui được gặp anh.
examples.translation: I'm David. Nice to meet you.

naturalMeaning: A polished, slightly formal sign-off you'll hear in service settings and see in writing.

examples.text: Cảm ơn quý khách, chúc một ngày tốt lành.
examples.translation: Thank you, have a nice day.

naturalMeaning: The standard thanks; add an address word to sound warmer, e.g. 'cảm ơn chị'.

examples.text: Cảm ơn, anh tốt quá.
examples.translation: Thank you, that's very kind of you.

naturalMeaning: 'Nhiều' ('a lot') strengthens the thanks; change 'anh' to fit the listener.

examples.text: Cảm ơn anh nhiều vì đã giúp tôi.
examples.translation: Thank you very much for helping me.

naturalMeaning: Both apologises and gets a stranger's attention; 'lỗi' takes the ngã (broken-rising) tone.

examples.text: Xin lỗi, cho tôi hỏi một chút.
examples.translation: Excuse me, may I ask you something?

naturalMeaning: The usual reply to 'cảm ơn'; literally 'there isn't anything'.

examples.text: Cảm ơn nhé! - Không có gì.
examples.translation: Thanks! - You're welcome.

naturalMeaning: Waves off an apology or reassures someone; 'không sao đâu' is a softer, warmer version.

examples.text: Xin lỗi anh! - Không sao.
examples.translation: Sorry! - It's ok.

naturalMeaning: Change 'anh' to suit the listener ('chị/em tên là gì?'). Literally 'you (are) named what?'.

examples.text: Anh tên là gì? - Tôi tên là Minh.
examples.translation: What's your name? - My name is Minh.

naturalMeaning: Neutral self-introduction; among friends you'd swap 'tôi' for 'mình' or 'em/anh'.

examples.text: Tôi tên là Anna.
examples.translation: My name is Anna.

naturalMeaning: Literally 'you are a person of which country?'; a very common opener with foreigners.

examples.text: Anh là người nước nào? - Tôi là người Anh.
examples.translation: What country are you from? - I'm from England.

naturalMeaning: Pattern: 'người' + country name. Swap 'Mỹ' (USA) for 'Anh' (UK), 'Úc' (Australia), etc.

examples.text: Tôi là người Mỹ, đến từ New York.
examples.translation: I'm American, from New York.

naturalMeaning: Literally 'are you well?'; a real question about health, not just a filler greeting. Adjust the address word.

examples.text: Lâu rồi không gặp, anh có khỏe không?
examples.translation: Long time no see, how are you?

naturalMeaning: Standard reply to 'có khỏe không?'; often followed by 'còn anh?' (and you?).

examples.text: Tôi khỏe, cảm ơn. Còn anh?
examples.translation: I'm fine, thanks. And you?

naturalMeaning: Literally 'you do what profession?'; a normal getting-to-know-you question.

examples.text: Anh làm nghề gì? - Tôi là giáo viên.
examples.translation: What do you do? - I'm a teacher.

naturalMeaning: The core market/shop question; you can point and just say it. Literally 'how much money?'.

examples.text: Cái áo này bao nhiêu tiền?
examples.translation: How much is this shirt?

naturalMeaning: 'Cái này' ('this one') plus a point of the finger handles almost any purchase.

examples.text: Cái này bao nhiêu? - Năm mươi nghìn.
examples.translation: How much is this? - Fifty thousand.

naturalMeaning: 'Quá' ('too') after an adjective is the go-to intensifier; the opening move of any bargain. (Southern speakers say 'mắc quá'.)

examples.text: Đắt quá! Bớt một chút đi.
examples.translation: Too expensive! Come down a bit.

naturalMeaning: '...được không?' ('is it possible?') is the polite way to request anything. Literally 'reduce the price, possible?'.

examples.text: Tôi mua hai cái, giảm giá được không?
examples.translation: I'll buy two, can you give a discount?

translation: can you take a little off?
naturalMeaning: 'Bớt' means to knock something off the price; 'một chút' ('a little') keeps it friendly.

examples.text: Bớt một chút được không chị?
examples.translation: Can you take a little off, please?

naturalMeaning: 'chỉ... thôi' frames 'only'; polite way to browse without committing to buy.

examples.text: Cảm ơn, tôi chỉ xem thôi.
examples.translation: Thanks, I'm just looking.

naturalMeaning: 'lấy' ('to take') is the natural verb for choosing an item at the point of sale.

examples.text: Được, tôi lấy cái này.
examples.translation: Ok, I'll take this one.

naturalMeaning: Cash and QR transfers still dominate; many small shops answer 'chỉ tiền mặt' (cash only).

examples.text: Trả bằng thẻ được không? - Được ạ.
examples.translation: Can I pay by card? - Yes.

naturalMeaning: Put the place first, then 'ở đâu?' ('is where?'): 'Khách sạn ở đâu?' = 'Where's the hotel?'.

examples.text: Bến xe buýt ở đâu?
examples.translation: Where is the bus stop?

naturalMeaning: 'Nhà vệ sinh' is the standard word; 'toilet' (borrowed) is also widely understood.

examples.text: Xin lỗi, nhà vệ sinh ở đâu?
examples.translation: Excuse me, where is the toilet?

naturalMeaning: A direction you mostly need to understand; often 'đi thẳng rồi rẽ...' (go straight then turn).

examples.text: Đi thẳng khoảng một trăm mét.
examples.translation: Go straight about a hundred metres.

naturalMeaning: Northern uses 'rẽ' for 'turn' (the South says 'quẹo'). 'Trái' = left.

examples.text: Đến ngã tư thì rẽ trái.
examples.translation: At the intersection, turn left.

naturalMeaning: 'Phải' = right (it also means 'must/correct', but context makes it clear).

examples.text: Rẽ phải là đến nơi.
examples.translation: Turn right and you're there.

naturalMeaning: 'Có... không?' brackets a yes/no question. Reply is often 'gần thôi' (it's close) or 'xa lắm' (very far).

examples.text: Từ đây đến hồ có xa không?
examples.translation: Is the lake far from here?

naturalMeaning: 'bị' marks something happening to you (usually unwanted); 'lạc' = to lose one's way.

examples.text: Tôi bị lạc, anh chỉ giúp tôi được không?
examples.translation: I'm lost, could you show me the way?

naturalMeaning: The single most useful learner phrase; 'hiểu' takes the hỏi tone.

examples.text: Xin lỗi, tôi không hiểu.
examples.translation: Sorry, I don't understand.

naturalMeaning: 'lại' signals repeating an action. Softer/fuller: 'anh nói lại được không?'.

examples.text: Xin nói lại một lần nữa.
examples.translation: Please say it again once more.

naturalMeaning: 'chậm' = slow, 'hơn' makes it 'more/-er'. A lifesaver in fast Hanoi conversation.

examples.text: Xin nói chậm hơn một chút.
examples.translation: Please speak a little more slowly.

naturalMeaning: 'tiếng Anh' = the English language (note 'Anh' here means England/English, not the pronoun).

examples.text: Anh nói tiếng Anh không? - Một chút thôi.
examples.translation: Do you speak English? - Just a little.

lemma: Cái này tiếng Việt gọi là gì?
romaji: kai nai tyeng vyet goy la zee
naturalMeaning: A great phrase for building vocabulary on the spot; literally 'this, in Vietnamese, is called what?'.

examples.text: Cái này tiếng Việt gọi là gì?
examples.translation: What's this called in Vietnamese?

naturalMeaning: 'đang' marks an action in progress. Saying this makes people slow down and cheer you on.

examples.text: Tôi đang học tiếng Việt nên nói còn chậm.
examples.translation: I'm learning Vietnamese, so I still speak slowly.

naturalMeaning: 'Cho tôi...' ('give me / let me...') is the workhorse of ordering. Many places use the borrowed word 'menu' too.

examples.text: Cho tôi xem thực đơn với.
examples.translation: May I see the menu, please?

naturalMeaning: Default is strong iced coffee with condensed milk ('cà phê sữa đá'); say 'cà phê đen' for black.

examples.text: Cho tôi một cà phê sữa đá.
examples.translation: One iced milk coffee, please.

naturalMeaning: 'bát' is the Northern word for bowl (the South says 'tô'). Specify 'phở bò' (beef) or 'phở gà' (chicken).

examples.text: Cho tôi một bát phở bò.
examples.translation: One bowl of beef pho, please.

naturalMeaning: Literally 'calculate the money'; call it out or catch the server's eye. Politer: 'Cho tôi tính tiền'.

examples.text: Em ơi, tính tiền!
examples.translation: Excuse me, the bill please!

naturalMeaning: The compliment every cook wants; 'quá' ('so/too') makes it heartfelt.

examples.text: Món này ngon quá!
examples.translation: This dish is so delicious!

naturalMeaning: 'mì chính' is the Northern word for MSG (the South says 'bột ngọt'); 'không cho' = don't add.

examples.text: Cho tôi bát phở, không cho mì chính nhé.
examples.translation: A bowl of pho for me, no MSG please.

naturalMeaning: 'ít' = little/less; handy for the very sweet drinks. Opposite is 'nhiều đường' (lots of sugar).

examples.text: Cho tôi cà phê ít đường thôi.
examples.translation: Coffee with just a little sugar, please.

naturalMeaning: 'cay' = spicy-hot; add 'nhé' to soften it into a friendly request.

examples.text: Cho tôi món này, không cay nhé.
examples.translation: This dish for me, not spicy please.

naturalMeaning: 'thêm' = more/additional; finish with the item, e.g. 'cho tôi thêm nước' (more water).

examples.text: Cho tôi thêm một cốc bia.
examples.translation: Give me one more glass of beer.

naturalMeaning: 'chai' = bottle, 'cốc' = glass, 'lon' = can. In Hanoi, 'bia hơi' (fresh draft beer) is the classic order.

examples.text: Cho tôi một chai bia Hà Nội.
examples.translation: One bottle of Hanoi beer, please.

naturalMeaning: Say it to a taxi/xe ôm driver and add the place; showing the address on your phone helps a lot.

examples.text: Cho tôi đến sân bay Nội Bài.
examples.translation: Take me to Noi Bai airport.

naturalMeaning: 'dừng' = to stop; tell the driver exactly where to pull over. Softer: 'cho tôi xuống ở đây' (let me off here).

examples.text: Dừng ở đây được rồi, cảm ơn.
examples.translation: Stop here, that's fine, thanks.

lemma: Đi đến đó bao nhiêu tiền?
romaji: dee den daw bow nyew tyen
naturalMeaning: Agree the fare before you get on a xe ôm; with metered/app rides it's already fixed.

examples.text: Đi đến phố cổ bao nhiêu tiền?
examples.translation: How much to go to the Old Quarter?

translation: I want to book a Grab
naturalMeaning: Grab (the ride-hailing app) is everywhere; 'đặt' = to book/order. 'Grab' is said as in English.

examples.text: Tôi muốn đặt Grab về khách sạn.
examples.translation: I want to book a Grab back to the hotel.

naturalMeaning: 'xe ôm' literally means 'hug vehicle' - you sit behind and hold on. Fast and cheap through Hanoi traffic.

examples.text: Gần thôi, mình đi xe ôm cho nhanh.
examples.translation: It's close, let's take a motorbike taxi to be quick.

translation: how long does it take to get there?
naturalMeaning: 'mất' here = 'takes (time)'; 'bao lâu' = how long. Reply often in 'phút' (minutes).

examples.text: Đến đó mất bao lâu? - Khoảng mười lăm phút.
examples.translation: How long does it take to get there? - About fifteen minutes.

naturalMeaning: 'mệt' covers both physically tired and worn out/unwell; 'mệt quá' = so tired.

examples.text: Hôm nay tôi mệt quá.
examples.translation: I'm so tired today.

naturalMeaning: 'đói' = hungry (sắc tone). Intensify with 'quá': 'đói quá'.

examples.text: Tôi đói rồi, mình đi ăn nhé.
examples.translation: I'm hungry now, let's go eat.

naturalMeaning: Fuller form is 'khát nước' ('thirsty for water'), which you'll also often hear.

examples.text: Trời nóng, tôi khát nước quá.
examples.translation: It's hot, I'm so thirsty.

naturalMeaning: 'vui' is happy/cheerful about a situation; the opposite is 'buồn' (sad).

examples.text: Gặp lại anh tôi rất vui.
examples.translation: I'm very glad to see you again.

naturalMeaning: Only for answering the phone - never a face-to-face greeting. Borrowed from French 'allô'.

examples.text: A lô, ai đấy ạ?
examples.translation: Hello, who's this?

naturalMeaning: Zalo is Vietnam's dominant messaging app; asking for it is how you swap contacts, like asking for WhatsApp elsewhere.

examples.text: Anh có Zalo không? Kết bạn nhé.
examples.translation: Do you have Zalo? Let's add each other.

naturalMeaning: 'gọi lại' = call back, 'sau' = later; the sentence-final 'nhé' makes it warm and friendly.

examples.text: Tôi đang bận, gọi lại sau nhé.
examples.translation: I'm busy right now, I'll call you back later.

naturalMeaning: The cry for help in a real emergency; 'cứu' = to rescue, 'với' adds urgency.

examples.text: Cứu với! Có người bị ngã!
examples.translation: Help! Someone has fallen!

naturalMeaning: 'cảnh sát' = police; the general emergency number in Vietnam is 113.

examples.text: Gọi cảnh sát đi, nhanh lên!
examples.translation: Call the police, quickly!

naturalMeaning: 'bệnh viện' = hospital; for a pharmacy ask for 'nhà thuốc'.

examples.text: Bệnh viện gần nhất ở đâu?
examples.translation: Where is the nearest hospital?

naturalMeaning: 'cần' = to need; 'bác sĩ' = doctor. Say it clearly in any medical emergency.

examples.text: Tôi cần bác sĩ, tôi thấy đau ngực.
examples.translation: I need a doctor, I have chest pain.

naturalMeaning: Tag '...được không?' onto any request to ask permission or possibility. 'Được' alone = 'ok/fine/it works'.

examples.text: Chụp ảnh ở đây được không?
examples.translation: Is it ok to take photos here?

naturalMeaning: The negative answer to a '...được không?' question; means 'can't / not allowed / no good'.

examples.text: Đỗ xe ở đây không được đâu.
examples.translation: You can't park here.

naturalMeaning: 'đồng ý' = to agree/consent; a clear yes to a proposal. To disagree: 'tôi không đồng ý'.

examples.text: Tôi đồng ý với anh.
examples.translation: I agree with you.

naturalMeaning: 'mấy giờ' = what hour, 'rồi' = already/now. To ask what time something happens: 'lúc mấy giờ?'.

examples.text: Bây giờ mấy giờ rồi?
examples.translation: What time is it now?

naturalMeaning: 'khi nào' asks 'when' about the future (put it first); 'mình' is the friendly inclusive 'we/us', 'nhau' = each other.

examples.text: Khi nào mình gặp nhau? - Chiều mai nhé.
examples.translation: When shall we meet? - Tomorrow afternoon.
